title: 1954 as a Turning Point
summary: Interviewed in Atlanta on September 16, 2011 by Joe Mosnier, with Henry Louis Gates auditing, Kathleen Neal Cleaver opens by naming 1954 as a turning point in her own life at age nine and in the world: the defeat of French imperialism in Vietnam and the Supreme Court's Brown decision throwing out separate but equal.

title: A Horror to the Segregationists
summary: She describes how the black community read Brown as making segregation unconstitutional, sparking spontaneous protests at libraries, pools, and buses, while it inspired viciousness in places like Mississippi and the murder of Emmett Till, and recalls her parents' civil rights activity at Tuskegee Institute before her father took a foreign aid post.

title: Point Four and a Childhood in India
summary: Cleaver explains her father was hired as a technical director under Truman's Point Four community development program in India, chosen for his rural farm work in Alabama, and how moving from a segregationist Alabama into newly independent India, the land of Nehru and Gandhi, showed her that dark-skinned people could govern themselves and blew apart white supremacist propaganda.

title: Growing Up Against Colonialism
topic: The Philippines and a Quaker School
summary: She recounts her father's transfer to the formerly colonized Philippines, growing up amid unfurling anti-colonial movements in Africa and Asia with the elimination of segregation and colonialism as her childhood norms, and coming back to attend a Society of Friends boarding school, the George School, in 1961, the same school Julian Bond had attended.

title: Girls in the Paddy Wagon
summary: As she neared graduation in May 1963 with Birmingham erupting and SNCC students jailed in Albany, Cleaver saw a Philadelphia newspaper photo of high school girls singing in the back of a paddy wagon being carted off to jail, wanted to be like them, posted a note proposing a dining-hall fast in support, and was rebuked by a classmate who urged a more cooperative solution.

title: Learning to Defend Nonviolence
topic: Fellowship House and an Assembly Speech
summary: Wanting to defend the students, Cleaver went to Fellowship House in Philadelphia seeking SNCC activist Prathia Hall, found her gone to Georgia, and got a resident artist to explain nonviolence as transformation through love and refusal to retaliate, then wrote and delivered a speech at her school's Thursday assembly defending the protesters and asking that they at least be respected.

title: Four Black Students in 400
summary: She describes the racial dynamics of a 400-student school with only four black students, how her speech upset the white audience of students and teachers who called her a radical, the follow-up discussions she organized, and her thwarted wish that summer of 1963 to attend the March on Washington for Jobs and Freedom.
related_events: March on Washington for Jobs and Freedom

title: Their Caution, Their History
summary: Cleaver reflects that her parents barred her from the March citing predicted violence and her father's caution as a foreign service officer, yet both had been activists: her father in the 1930s effort to desegregate the Texas white primary, her mother in the Southern Negro Youth Congress and a beneficiary of a Virginia consent decree paying her graduate tuition at Michigan, before she went off to Oberlin College.

title: A Hotbed at Oberlin
summary: At Oberlin she found an active black-and-white civil rights group, radical faculty, and speakers describing protests like the one in Danville, Virginia, and lived through the 16th Street Baptist Church bombing and President Kennedy's assassination, with friends fearing what the struggle would become under Lyndon Johnson, whom young people never expected to back civil rights.

title: Reports in Triplicate
summary: Feeling college was not what she had imagined, Cleaver quit and took Washington government jobs, moving from the Peace Corps to the new Community Relations Service set up under the 1964 Civil Rights Act, where she typed in triplicate the reports field teams filed after conflicts like the Cleveland riots; she also recalls Malcolm X's February murder and a night her parents took her to a Baltimore mosque advertising a Malcolm X and James Baldwin debate, where the Muslims at the door nearly refused her as white and it proved to be only a taped film.

title: A Circle of Future Leaders
summary: Cleaver describes her year at the Community Relations Service under deputy director Roger Wilkins, a civil rights attorney who knew her father from his post in Sierra Leone, and reconnecting with James Thomas, a Crossroads Africa student turned law student who had once invited her as a high schooler to his first trial at Yale Law School, where she met fellow students Marian Wright Edelman and Eleanor Holmes Norton, an inspiring circle in which no one close to her opposed the movement.

title: Meeting George Ware
summary: Wanting New York, Cleaver enrolled at Barnard but quickly found the classes dull, and that summer met George Ware of SNCC, who had come out of the Tuskegee student movement and led a group called TIAL active in the Selma and Tuskegee marches, introduced through a graduate student she was dating whose mother had taught in Tuskegee and knew her mother.

title: Too Collaborative for George
summary: In a transformative first conversation, George Ware told Cleaver how the Tuskegee Civic Association in 88-percent-black Macon County ran only two candidates rather than contest an all-black council, which he found horrible, and how TIAL deliberately integrated white churches to provoke the armed parents and break up an overly collaborative approach; Ware, a brilliant chemist from rural Alabama who talked only of activism, became her mentor as she enrolled at Barnard while he took a chemistry job at Hoffmann-La Roche and her boyfriend Ernest worked in a New York lab near the Columbia campus.

title: A Job at SNCC
summary: Through Ivanhoe Donaldson, a 1964 Mississippi summer project director she met at her friends' apartment, Cleaver got a thrilling 1966 summer secretary job at the New York SNCC office, a fundraising office where Southern activists came for rest, dental work, or because they had burned out, arriving just about two weeks after the call for black power in Greenwood, Mississippi.

part: Black Power and SNCC in Turmoil
title: Asleep a Chairman, Awake Not
summary: Cleaver recounts that white staff in leadership had been asked to leave SNCC, the New York director Elizabeth Sutherland being replaced by Ivanhoe Donaldson amid the turmoil as black power supplanted freedom now after the Voting Rights Act, and how a second vote at a midnight 1966 staff meeting replaced John Lewis as chairman with Stokely Carmichael, so Lewis went to sleep chairman and woke to find he was not.

part: Black Power and SNCC in Turmoil
title: Shot in the Back
topic: The Murder of Sammy Younge Jr.
summary: Cleaver tells of Sammy Younge Jr., a Navy veteran she knew from childhood who returned to Tuskegee and became an extraordinarily active organizer working with George Ware and others on an independent black party in Lowndes County, and who in January was shot in the back and murdered at a gas station by attendant Marvin Segrest while registering black voters, a death devastating to SNCC.

part: Black Power and SNCC in Turmoil
title: Julian Bond and the War
summary: She recounts how Younge's murder led George Ware to quit his chemistry job and return to the movement to run the Atlanta campus program, and how, because Younge was a veteran, SNCC took a position against the Vietnam War that Julian Bond endorsed, leading Georgia to deny Bond his elected legislative seat in a fight that reached the Supreme Court.

part: Black Power and SNCC in Turmoil
title: SNCC in Crisis
summary: At the end of January 1967 Cleaver moved to Atlanta to work for Ware, finding SNCC financially broke with projects eliminated and direct action giving way to black power, and recalls the SNCC newsletter, researched by Jack Minnis and written by Ethel Minor, that criticized Israeli warfare techniques and the cluster bombs that looked like toys, which horrified Jewish donors during the 1967 war.

part: Black Power and SNCC in Turmoil
title: Three Days Taking Notes
topic: Forman, and Trading Tuition for SNCC
summary: Cleaver describes a 14-hour meeting where she did nothing but take notes, the report that became Black Power: The Politics of Liberation, and her deep admiration for James Forman, whom she remembered from a riveting Jet Magazine photo facing a Mississippi sheriff toe to toe, and tells how she wrote her father that SNCC was a better education than Barnard and asked for the tuition money instead, which funded the rent on a single Atlanta apartment shared by the whole campus program as an unpaid cadre that hitchhiked to work for lack of gas money.

part: Liberation Will Come From a Black Thing
title: A Conference of Black Students
summary: Cleaver recounts organizing a SNCC conference called Liberation Will Come From a Black Thing to bring radical black student groups together at Fisk University, capturing the new black-student consciousness then emerging, and sending invitations to speakers including LeRoi Jones, Margaret Walker, Charles V. Hamilton, and Eldridge Cleaver, who had published in Ramparts.

part: Liberation Will Come From a Black Thing
title: Stark, Raving Mad
summary: She describes the Nashville press going wild because the SNCC chairman was reviled and threatened with deportation as a native of Trinidad, the Vanderbilt Impact symposium controversy over inviting him alongside Strom Thurmond and others, and Fisk deciding to kick the conference off campus, leaving the organizers with arriving students, no rooms, and an Easter-weekend blizzard shutting the East Coast airports.

part: Liberation Will Come From a Black Thing
title: A Giant in the Doorway
summary: With speakers stranded by the blizzard, only Eldridge Cleaver made it from California and a local Episcopal pastor, Father Woodruff, offered his church off campus, saving the conference; Cleaver recalls retyping the agenda when this giant out of prison about three months was brought in, masklike and almost intimidating, asking her for a room and a typewriter to get her attention, how she beat him at chess back in Atlanta and the two fell in love, and the huge Nashville riot that erupted around the Easter conference as police drilled in riot control began rioting before Carmichael even arrived.

part: Liberation Will Come From a Black Thing
title: All the Preparation for a Revolutionary
summary: She reflects that though she would not have called herself a revolutionary as a Barnard sophomore, she already had all the preparation, needing only a few more pieces of experience, which came when she met Eldridge Cleaver, a Marxist who kept that to himself and held a more radical revolutionary model than SNCC, on parole and confined by his officer to San Francisco's city limits.

title: A Self-Selected Radical Crowd
summary: Cleaver recalls the amazing collection of radical student groups that did reach the conference, including the Cornell black student group later famous for the armed Straight Hall photographs and groups from Berkeley and Roosevelt University, and frames SNCC as a debating society, ideological institute, family, and activist group in transition toward a revolutionary movement it could not sustain as an intact organization.

title: Integration Is Disintegration
summary: She lays out the first black power argument she absorbed, from Carmichael's Black Power article, that integration deals only with color and not the black community's real problem, the absence of power and institutions, and recounts going out in July to visit Eldridge Cleaver, joking they would send Willie Ricks to retrieve her, while he, forbidden on parole to associate with armed people, drove her to Oakland to meet the Black Panthers in the summer of love of 1967.

title: From SNCC to Liberation
topic: A Movement Gravitates to the Panthers
summary: She traces how black power funneled SNCC veterans into the black liberation movement of the late sixties, naming early Black Panther leaders who had been in SNCC, and recounts Eldridge Cleaver's arrest after an Oakland police shootout in which one Panther was killed, his parole revoked, and his unbelievable June 1968 release on a habeas petition, his nomination as the Peace and Freedom Party candidate, and the publication of Soul on Ice.

title: We Could See the War
summary: Cleaver describes how the Black Panther world outlook was shaped by a Vietnam War they could watch on television, taken as evidence of a reckless imperial United States, and how the Vietnamese proved an organized people could defeat a richer power, citing the reported remark of General Giap that the Americans won every battle and it did not matter.

title: Ending Segregation Was Not Integration
summary: Returning after a break, Cleaver argues the movement has been taught wrong, that ending school segregation was about stopping oppressive exclusion rather than merging separate societies, that integration was a government idea not from the black community, and that the real story was a groundswell of unnamed local people after the Montgomery bus boycott she defines as community revolution.

title: That Mess Becomes the Movement
topic: How Communities Turned to the Struggle
summary: Cleaver criticizes the academic framing that treats the movement as good until King died and bad afterward because black power was cast as anti-white, recalls that in the late fifties many called the movement that mess, and contrasts community structures thick with churches and colleges against Oakland's loosely organized openness, which drew young people who flocked into the Black Panther Party because being a Panther became the thing to do.

title: Civil Rights and Black Power Are One
summary: She insists treating civil rights as separate from black power is a mistake when the shared issue was how excluded people empower themselves, surveys the spectrum from SNCC to SCLC to the NAACP, tells how Fred Hampton had led an NAACP youth council and as an elementary student already fed hungry classmates breakfast years before the Panthers' free breakfast program, and describes a world repudiating colonialism with successful revolutions in China and Cuba as proof ordinary people could change their lives, the FBI-planted black propaganda demonizing the Panthers, and the founding of the Black Panther Party for Self-Defense in Oakland in October 1966, named from the Lowndes County Freedom Organization's panther logo.

title: Panthers Across the Country
summary: She explains how the panther name passed from Alabama to Oakland and became a symbol of black power, with groups forming in urban areas nationwide, including a Kansas City group under Pete O'Neal that split off as the Sons of Malcolm, alongside the Nation of Islam, black elected officials, and white SDS and anti-war activists in a general ferment.

title: Finding a Counter
summary: Cleaver argues the state used its posture toward genuine communist enemies to paint the radical Panthers, sending James Farmer of CORE to Africa to counter Malcolm X's alignments there, and recounts setting up an international section of the Black Panther Party in Algeria with Eldridge Cleaver, meeting the African and Arab revolutionaries the intelligence agencies most feared they would ally with.

title: The Rise of a Black Messiah
summary: She describes the FBI's lists of key extremists and a counterintelligence program against every form of activism, the domestic COINTELPRO begun around 1967 whose objective was to prevent the rise of a black messiah who could unify the black masses, and its early targets: Max Stanford of the Revolutionary Action Movement, Stokely Carmichael, and above all Martin Luther King with his Nobel Prize and worldwide following.

title: Scare You or Kill You
topic: Bobby Seale and a Frightened State
summary: Cleaver explains the Panthers were not initially on the FBI's list partly because the party was collective, describes the attempt that wounded Bobby Seale in a shootout that left a policeman dead and his resulting murder charge, recounts proposing courthouse demonstrations, starting with about eight people, that grew into an international movement, and reflects that what frightened the government was encountering armed, articulate black people who told authorities what they would not do, an experience drawn from colonial warfare that they faced knowing they risked their lives.

title: A Twentieth-Century Set of Demands
topic: A Young Spokeswoman and the Ten Point Platform
summary: Responding to the interviewer's framing of her young self as part of the apparatus explaining the party amid rapid ideological shifts and law-enforcement pressure in Oakland, Cleaver notes she was actually older than most high-school-age Panthers, then describes how the Black Panther Party for Self-Defense started with a tiny Oakland group but addressed the whole black community, its Ten Point Platform essentially reform analogous to the Atlanta student movement's demands and the colored people's conventions after the Civil War, a twentieth-century version of an oppressed community's hopes in new words.

title: I Wouldn't Let Anybody Beat Me
summary: Cleaver recounts that what drove many older activists into the movement was Emmett Till's 1955 murder, while the Panthers drew slightly older youth, many children and grandchildren of Southern migrants in big cities for whom televised beatings in Birmingham were traumatizing rather than mere news, citing Emory Douglas's resolve that no one would beat him like that, which the Panthers' self-defense stance answered.

title: A Generation That Believed It Was Immortal
summary: She characterizes the Panthers as a collective youth movement whose members believed themselves immortal and were undeterred by the real danger of being killed, backed by adults who would not join but would help, and recounts a story from Assata Shakur's autobiography of a friend who sheltered her without surprise as her picture circulated for a bank robbery, illustrating people who quietly helped.

title: Democracy for the White, Tyranny for the Black
summary: Cleaver describes the flip side, people pressed to inform on activists to alienate their support, the surveillance reaching even her son, whose name she found in the Mississippi sovereignty commission records though she doubts he was ever there, and likens the United States to a South African sociologist's description of a society with democracy for the white and tyranny for the black.

title: The Distance of Forty Years
summary: Asked how forty years of distance has changed the telling, Cleaver considers how the immediacy and ugliness of what they confronted reads differently now, reflects on the Black Power Mixtape film and why these images resonate, recalls a 1970 Black Panther newspaper issue on fascism filled with page after page of arrests, citing Mumia Abu-Jamal jailed as a teenager for jaywalking, and frames the everyday police raids, bombed offices, and FBI hostility as low-intensity counterinsurgency warfare targeted at people who understood themselves as revolutionaries accepting the risk of war.

title: A Different Country We Won't Be
summary: Cleaver closes on how the intensity has diminished because so many powerful, brilliant figures of that time, from singer Miriam Makeba to Stokely Carmichael, have passed, imagining how different the country would be had Malcolm X, Martin Luther King, and Fred Hampton not been killed and their organizations flourished, and noting that the Black Power Mixtape appeals because it shows young, charming, upbeat people who still made room to live and party.
